title: Listening - Part 4: Working as an Airline Pilot
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Molly, who flies passenger planes. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today we’re meeting Molly James, who flies passenger planes for a regional airline. Molly, had you always wanted to become a pilot? || Molly: Not from a very young age. My father repaired airport vehicles, so I knew something about aviation, but I imagined I would become a teacher. [24]It was only after a flying lesson given to me for my eighteenth birthday that I seriously considered piloting as a career[/24]. I loved having to think ahead while also enjoying the view. || Presenter: Did your family encourage you? || Molly: They were realistic about how hard it would be and how much training would cost, but they were never negative. [25]My parents said that if flying was what interested me most, being a woman should not stop me trying[/25]. That support mattered, since I rarely saw female pilots when I was training. || Presenter: How did you get your first licence? || Molly: A private pilot’s licence requires lessons and a certain number of flying hours. I didn’t have enough money straight after school, so [26]I worked in a hotel for a year to pay for the lessons I needed[/26]. It wasn’t my dream job, but I knew exactly why I was doing it. || Presenter: Is the commercial licence simply the next exam? || Molly: I wish it were! There are written tests, medical checks and many hours in different aircraft. [27]You cannot apply for a full commercial licence until you have completed a considerable amount of additional flight training[/27]. It takes time, and people should plan their finances carefully. || Presenter: You are now a first officer. What does that mean in practice? || Molly: People sometimes assume I only watch the captain, but that is not true. We prepare the route together, and on different flights we divide duties. [28]When it is my turn, I fly the aircraft while the captain handles radio communication and checks[/28]. Responsibility is shared, with the captain having overall command. || Presenter: What is the next step in your career? || Molly: I hope to become a senior first officer soon. The attraction is not just a different title: [29]senior status allows me to operate on a wider range of routes and land at more airports[/29]. That variety would build my experience before I eventually apply to be a captain. || Presenter: What advice do you give people considering this career? || Molly: Flying is wonderful if it is genuinely what you want, but the glamorous image is misleading. [30]Training is expensive, and new pilots do not always earn enough immediately to repay what they borrowed[/30]. Research the costs, speak to working pilots and only then make a decision.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What does Molly say about her decision to become a pilot?

options.A: She made it after experiencing flying herself.
options.B: She was influenced by a teacher at school.
options.C: It had been her ambition since childhood.

questions.explanation: She only began considering the career seriously after an eighteenth-birthday flying lesson.

questions.question: Molly's parents

options.A: would have preferred her to become a teacher.
options.B: believed that her gender should not affect her choice.
options.C: were concerned about her working for an airline.

questions.explanation: They told her being a woman should not prevent her from trying to become a pilot.

questions.question: What did Molly do in order to get her private pilot’s licence?

options.A: She got a job to finance her lessons.
options.C: She asked her father to train her.

questions.explanation: She worked in a hotel for a year to pay for lessons.

questions.question: What does Molly say about getting a full commercial pilot’s licence?

options.A: It depends mainly on passing medical checks.
options.B: It is available soon after the private licence.
options.C: It requires a large amount of further training.

questions.explanation: Molly says substantial additional flight training must be completed first.

questions.question: What does Molly say about being first officer?

options.A: She occasionally takes responsibility for flying the plane.
options.B: She deals with radio communication on every flight.
options.C: She makes decisions without the captain's involvement.

questions.explanation: When it is her turn, Molly flies while the captain handles communications and checks.

questions.question: What is an advantage of becoming a senior first officer?

options.A: She would be paid while completing training.
options.B: She could gain experience on more routes.
options.C: She would become responsible for training captains.

questions.explanation: Senior status allows her to use more airports and fly a wider range of routes.

questions.question: What warning does Molly give about becoming a pilot?

options.A: The first jobs may not cover the cost of training quickly.
options.B: Only people with family connections find airline work.
options.C: It is difficult to obtain reliable career information.

questions.explanation: She warns that training is costly and beginning salaries may not quickly repay borrowing.
